title: Psalms 22
subtitle: My God, My God, Why Have You Forsaken Me?

heb.paragraph: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me (ʿăzabtānî)?" (v.1). Jesus quotes this from the cross (Matt 27:46). The most christological psalm in the Psalter. Every detail of the crucifixion is anticipated: scorned and despised (v.6), mocked by those who shake their heads (v.7), "he trusts in the LORD — let the LORD rescue him" (v.8), bulls of Bashan surround (v.12), poured out like water, bones out of joint (v.14), heart like wax (v.14), strength dried up (v.15), tongue sticks to the roof of the mouth (v.15), pierced hands and feet (v.16), they divide garments and cast lots for clothing (v.18).

"They pierced (kārū) my hands and my feet" (v.16). The most debated word in the psalm. The Masoretic text reads "like a lion" (kaʾari); the LXX and Dead Sea Scrolls support "they pierced" (kārū). The Christian tradition reads "pierced" as a crucifixion prophecy.

refs.note: Jesus quotes Psalm 22:1 from the cross: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?"
"They divided his garments among them by casting lots" — fulfilling Psalm 22:18.
"Those who passed by hurled insults, shaking their heads... He trusts in God. Let God rescue him" — fulfilling Psalm 22:7–8.
"I will declare your name to my brothers" — quoting Psalm 22:22 for Christ’s solidarity with humanity.

echoes.source_context: The psalmist's cry of abandonment: 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' — a psalm that moves from desolation to praise, from isolation to restoration.
echoes.connection: Jesus quotes this opening line from the cross in Aramaic: 'Eloi, Eloi, lema sabachthani?' The only time in the Gospels where Jesus addresses God without the intimate 'Father.'
echoes.significance: By quoting the psalm's opening, Jesus invokes the entire psalm — including its resolution in vindication and praise (vv.22–31). The cry of dereliction is simultaneously the deepest point of suffering and a prophetic declaration that God will yet act. The psalm's details (pierced hands, divided garments, mocking bystanders) map onto the crucifixion with extraordinary precision.

echoes.source_context: The psalmist laments: 'They divide my clothes among them and cast lots for my garment.'
echoes.connection: John reports that the soldiers divided Jesus' outer garments into four shares and cast lots for his seamless undergarment, then explicitly notes: 'This happened that the scripture might be fulfilled.'
echoes.significance: John sees the soldiers' mundane act of dividing spoils as the fulfillment of a thousand-year-old psalm. The specificity of the detail (casting lots for a single garment) made this one of the early church's most compelling prophecy-fulfillment texts.

notes.note: "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" Jesus’ cry from the cross. The ultimate forsakenness: God-from-God, separated by sin-bearing.
"I am a worm and not a man, scorned by everyone. All who see me mock me: ‘He trusts in the LORD — let the LORD rescue him.’" The mockery at Calvary scripted 1,000 years beforehand.

"I am poured out like water; all my bones are out of joint; my heart has turned to wax. My strength is dried up. They pierce my hands and my feet. They divide my clothes and cast lots for my garment." The crucifixion in detail — written centuries before crucifixion was invented.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the body-language of suffering is forensically precise: dislocated joints, melted heart, parched throat, pierced limbs. The poem forces the reader to witness what the sufferer endures. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.
Calvin: Christ’s use of this psalm from the cross proves its prophetic nature. Calvin: David’s suffering was real but Christ’s was the ultimate fulfilment. The type points to the antitype.
Calvin: the lot-casting for garments fulfils the psalm with mathematical precision. Calvin: Scripture does not merely predict; it scripts.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ʿăzabtānî (you have forsaken me) is a strong word for abandonment. The same verb describes a husband leaving a wife (Isa 54:6) or a city left desolate (Isa 62:4).
The NET discusses the textual debate: MT "like a lion" (kaʾari) vs "they pierced" (kārū). The Dead Sea Scrolls (Nahal Hever) support "pierced." Most Christian translations follow this reading.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: Jesus did not merely quote this psalm; he entered it. The forsakenness was real, not theatrical. The psalm became biography.

Kidner: crucifixion was not practised in David’s era. The description of pierced extremities, visible bones, and divided garments transcends any experience David could have had. The Spirit spoke beyond the speaker.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: Psalm 22 is the Psalter’s most sustained christological text. The NT applies it to Christ more comprehensively than any other psalm. The suffering described is both David’s and, supremely, Christ’s. VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: the opening cry is not despair but prayer. David addresses God twice: "my God, my God." The relationship persists through the forsakenness. You can only be forsaken by someone you belong to. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: David’s lament begins with the cry Jesus made his own. The sufferer is scorned (v.6), mocked (vv.7–8), surrounded by enemies described as bulls, lions, and dogs (vv.12–16). The physical suffering is detailed with medical precision: dehydration (v.15), skeletal visibility (v.17), pierced extremities (v.16). Garments divided, lots cast (v.18). Every element was fulfilled at Calvary.

sections.header: Verses 19–31 — “All the Ends of the Earth Will Turn to the LORD”

heb.gloss: they will proclaim / they will tell
heb.paragraph: "They will proclaim (sipprū) his righteousness, declaring to a people yet unborn: He has done it!" (v.31). The psalm’s last words: "he has done it" — in Hebrew a single word that parallels Jesus’ "it is finished" (John 19:30). The work is complete. The suffering produces universal proclamation.

refs.note: "It is finished" (tetelestai) — parallels "he has done it!" in Psalm 22:31.
"I will declare your name to my brothers" — quoting Psalm 22:22 for Christ’s identification with humanity.
"A great multitude from every nation" — the fulfilment of "all the ends of the earth will turn to the LORD."
"Every knee should bow" — the cosmic worship Psalm 22:27–29 anticipates.

notes.note: "I will declare your name to my people." Hebrews 2:12 applies this to Christ: after the suffering comes proclamation. The one who was forsaken becomes the preacher.

"All the ends of the earth will remember and turn to the LORD, and all the families of the nations will bow down before him, for dominion belongs to the LORD." The vision: universal worship. From one man’s suffering to all nations’ salvation.
"He has done it!" The psalm’s final declaration. The work is complete. The parallels to "it is finished" (John 19:30) are unmistakable.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the pivot from agony to praise is abrupt — no buffer, no gradual recovery. One moment forsaken, the next proclaiming. The literary structure mimics the theology: death to life without mediation. Alter's literary translation draws attention to the poetic artistry of the Hebrew — rhythm, parallelism, sound play, and imagery that constitute not merely decoration but meaning. The form of the poem enacts its content: how the words are arranged is inseparable from what the words say.
Calvin: the universal worship vision proves the psalm’s scope exceeds David. Calvin: no event in David’s life produced worldwide worship. Only the cross did that.
Calvin: "he has done it" is the gospel in one word. Calvin: the entire plan of salvation — from eternal decree to Calvary — is summarised in this single declaration of completion.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: the shift from lament to praise is marked by "I will declare" — the vow of praise, common in lament psalms, where the sufferer promises to testify after rescue.
The NET: ʿasâ (he has done it) is often compared to Jesus’ tetelestai (it is finished) in John 19:30. Both declare completed work.

kidner.source: Derek Kidner, Psalms, TOTC (2 vols., 1973/1975) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Kidner: the shift from suffering to praise is the psalm’s most remarkable feature. There is no transition marker — suffering suddenly becomes proclamation. This mirrors the cross: death becomes resurrection without explanation, only event. Kidner's expository approach combines close attention to the Hebrew text with pastoral application. His reading identifies the psalm's enduring relevance: the same theological realities that shaped Israel's worship continue to address the human condition with clarity and power.

vangemeren.source: Willem A. VanGemeren, Psalms, EBC Rev. (2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: VanGemeren: the universal vision (all nations worship) connects to Psalm 2’s promise (nations as the Son’s inheritance). The messianic psalms form a network: suffering (Ps 22) fulfils enthronement (Ps 2). VanGemeren situates the psalm within the Psalter's canonical structure, noting how its themes connect to surrounding compositions. The Psalter is not a random anthology but a curated theological argument — each psalm contributes to the whole, and its placement shapes how it should be read and prayed.

goldingay.source: John Goldingay, Psalms, BCOT (3 vols., 2006–2008) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Goldingay: "he has done it" is one word in Hebrew: ʿasâ. Completed action. The work is finished. The declaration is as terse as it is monumental. Goldingay reads the psalm within the context of Israel's worship life, attending to its liturgical setting and its function as communal prayer. The psalm speaks both for the individual and for the congregation — personal experience is given theological significance through the grammar of corporate worship.

hist.context: The psalm pivots from lament to praise. David asks for rescue (vv.19–21), then declares: "I will declare your name to my people; in the assembly I will praise you" (v.22 — quoted in Heb 2:12). The vision expands: all the families of nations will worship (vv.27–28), future generations will be told about the Lord (v.30), and the psalm closes with "he has done it!" (v.31) — the OT’s "it is finished."

ppl.name: David
ppl.role: King of Israel; speaking prophetically as the Suffering Servant
ppl.text: Psalm 22 transcends David’s experience. Every detail — forsakenness, mockery, piercing, lot-casting — finds precise fulfilment in Christ’s crucifixion. Jesus quoted it from the cross.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVYet you are enthroned as the Holy One; you are the one Israel praises.ESVYet you are holy, enthroned on the praises of Israel.NIV“He trusts in the Lord,” they say, “let the Lord rescue him. Let him deliver him, since he delights in him.”ESV\u201cHe trusts in the LORD; let him deliver him; let him rescue him, for he delights in him!\u201d

rec.text: ""My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" Jesus’ cry from the cross. The ultimate forsakenness: God-from-God, separated by sin-bearing."

rows.label: The Triumph — 22:19–31
rows.text: From agony to praise. "All nations will bow." "He has done it!" — the OT’s "It is finished."

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: the most detailed crucifixion prophecy in the OT. Mission at 8: all nations will worship. The psalm IS the gospel in miniature: suffering → death → proclamation → universal worship.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on the cry 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' consider asking God whether you can hold together the agony of abandonment and the hope of future praise — and whether this psalm's journey from despair to worship mirrors your own.
